sourceMovie.title: Treasure Planet
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Treasure Planet (2002) as a science fiction theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The science fiction and adventure blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Atlantis: The Lost Empire
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Treasure Planet who respond to its science-fiction will find Atlantis: The Lost Empire (2001) immediately familiar. Both films share a adventure sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Home
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Treasure Planet fans keep landing on Home (2015) — the science-fiction is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Atlantis: Milo's Return
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Atlantis: Milo's Return draws from the same science-fiction well as Treasure Planet, channeling animation in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2003 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: The Sea Beast
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Sea Beast (2022) captures the adventure appeal of Treasure Planet without simply imitating it. The animation streak running through both films is what puts The Sea Beast at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: James and the Giant Peach
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The adventure appeal of Treasure Planet — that uplifting edge, the animation — translates cleanly into James and the Giant Peach (1996). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Titan A.E.
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Titan A.E. (2000) clearly understood what makes science-fiction work — the same quality that keeps Treasure Planet rewatchable. Expect the same uplifting rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Time Bandits
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Time Bandits (1981) is the kind of film Treasure Planet fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The science-fiction overlap is real, but it's the funny emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Moana
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Moana shares Treasure Planet's gift for adventure — the 2016 film delivers animation without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: The Iron Giant
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Treasure Planet compelling — the science-fiction, the animation — shows up in The Iron Giant (1999) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that uplifting storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Peter Pan
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The adventure energy of Treasure Planet is alive in Peter Pan (2003), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a uplifting payoff that rivals the original pick.

faq.question: What should I watch after Treasure Planet?
faq.answer: Try Atlantis: The Lost Empire, Home, Atlantis: Milo's Return—each shares DNA with Treasure Planet while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Treasure Planet?
faq.answer: Treasure Planet (2002) is best described as Science Fiction, Adventure, Animation, Family, Fantasy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Treasure Planet worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.6/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this science fiction lane.
